front: Novel: the narrator recalls complimenting a man he 'disapproved of from beginning to end' by shouting 'You're worth the whole damn bunch put together'
Novel: ends with the line 'So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past'
Novel: the title character is shot in his swimming pool after his yellow car is involved in a fatal hit-and-run in the Valley of Ashes
Novel: a shady character wears cufflinks made of human molars and is infamous for fixing the 1919 World Series
back: Meyer Wolfsheim in The Great Gatsby (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Novel: the epigraph 'Then Wear the Gold Hat' is attributed to the fictional poet Thomas Parke D'Invilliers
Novel: alternate titles for this work included 'The High-bouncing Lover' and 'Trimalchio'
Novel: the editor Maxwell Perkins advised adding the catchphrase 'old sport' and praised the 'great unblinking eyes of T.J. Eckleberg'
Novel: a character inspired by both Ginevra King and Zelda Sayre hopes her daughter will be 'a beautiful little fool'
back: Daisy Buchanan in The Great Gatsby (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Novel: Nicole's mental health struggles mirror the author's wife; the couple meets at a Swiss mental asylum and moves to the French Riviera
back: Tender Is the Night (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Novel: the protagonist, Amory Blaine, distinguishes between 'the slicker' and 'the big man' at Princeton
back: This Side of Paradise (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Novel: ends with a generation 'grown up to find all gods dead, all wars fought, all faiths in man shaken'
back: This Side of Paradise (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Novel: the car crash death of Princeton student Dick Humbird, inspired by a real crash with Hinman Bird
back: This Side of Paradise (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Short Story: a golf caddy becomes infatuated with a woman, then later reflects 'now that thing is gone' upon learning she married the philanderer Lud Simms
Short Story: the protagonist learns the English method of washing wool golf socks and opens a laundry empire across the Midwest
Short Story: Percy Washington boasts his father is the richest man in the world; part of the 'Gatsby cluster' of five stories
back: The Diamond as Big as the Ritz (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Short Story: the protagonist claims to be a 'society vampire,' gets a scandalous haircut, then cuts off her cousin Marjorie's pigtails in revenge
back: Bernice Bobs Her Hair (F. Scott Fitzgerald)

front: Short Story: Sally Carrol nearly freezes to death after being abandoned by her northern beau Harry inside the title structure
Short Story: an Art Nouveau title object causes misfortunes including revealing an affair and causing a daughter to lose her hand
Short Story: Father Schwartz collapses after hearing Rudolph Miller's confession; developed from a discarded early draft of The Great Gatsby
Author: essay begins 'Of course all life is a process of breaking down' and proposes that 'the test of a first-rate intelligence is the ability to hold two opposed ideas in the mind'
Author: wrote in notebooks 'Show me a hero and I'll write you a tragedy'
Author: Irwin Shaw said 'the ghost of [this writer] hangs over every American typewriter'
